key_insight: Fair, like-for-like benchmarking shows that the best inverse-solver choice depends mainly on how the unknown is represented: grid fields favor adjoint methods, while neural representations favor PINNs.
engineering_relevance: For PDE-constrained material and parameter identification, this comparison helps engineers choose the solver that best matches the physics representation, cost constraints, and time-dependence of the problem.
method: Unified both methods under the same PDEs, domains, observation models, regularization, optimizer, and precision.
Tested on Burgers, Darcy permeability inversion, Allen–Cahn reaction identification, and Navier–Stokes viscosity identification.
Compared discrete adjoint optimization against PINNs, including a PINN-warm-started adjoint hybrid.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports benchmark evaluations across four inverse-problem cases, showing different performance regimes by parameterization and that warm-starting PINNs can reduce adjoint cost while preserving adjoint-level accuracy.
watch_item: Results depend strongly on the chosen unknown representation and on practical factors such as trajectory storage, differentiation overhead, and fair optimizer/regularization matching.
paper_title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
